title: Vegan Air Fryer Recipes: Quick, Crispy & Plant-Based
alt: A collection of easy vegan air fryer recipes including tofu, calzones, donuts, and more
description: The best vegan air fryer recipes all in one place — crispy tofu, calzones, donuts, curries, and more. Quick, plant-based, and no deep frying needed.
content:
## Why the Air Fryer Is a Vegan Game Changer

If you have not cooked tofu in an air fryer yet, you are missing out. The air fryer gives you that crispy exterior without deep frying, which means less oil, less mess, and less time standing over a stove. It is one of the best tools in a vegan kitchen.

> The air fryer is a vegan game changer — crispy tofu, golden donuts, and flaky calzones with a fraction of the oil and in half the time. Once you start using it, it is hard to go back.

Beyond tofu, the air fryer handles donuts, calzones, wraps, and more. Here are all our vegan air fryer recipes in one place.

## The Recipes

[recipes:Air Fryer]

## Tips for Best Results

### Pressing Tofu

Always press your tofu for at least 15 minutes before air frying. The drier the tofu, the crispier it gets.

### Oil and Spray

A light spray of neutral oil (avocado or sunflower) helps everything crisp up evenly.

### Temperature Guide

Most tofu and savory recipes work well at 375–400°F for 15–20 minutes, flipping halfway. Doughs like calzones and donuts do better at 350°F.

## FAQs

**Q: Can you cook tofu in the air fryer without oil?**

A: Yes! The tofu will still get crispy without oil, especially if it is well-pressed.

**Q: What vegan proteins work best in the air fryer?**

A: Extra-firm tofu is the star, but you can also air fry tempeh, vegan chicken strips, and even canned chickpeas.

**Q: Do I need to preheat the air fryer?**

A: Most models benefit from a 3-minute preheat, but it is not always required.

title: 10 Essential Vegan Pantry Ingredients
alt: A spread of key vegan cooking ingredients including nutritional yeast, tahini, miso, and legumes
description: Stock these 10 ingredients and you can make almost any vegan meal from scratch — from creamy sauces and hearty stews to rich desserts and umami-packed bowls.
content:
Building a vegan pantry can feel overwhelming at first, but it comes down to a handful of powerhouse ingredients. Master these and you'll never be stuck at dinner time.

[listicle:Nutritional Yeast|🧀|The vegan umami bomb|/images/creamy-vegan-cauliflower-basil-soup.jpg]

Nutritional yeast — affectionately called "nooch" — is a deactivated yeast with a cheesy, nutty, savory flavour. It's loaded with B vitamins (often including B12 when fortified) and adds depth to anything it touches.

**Use it in:** Cheese sauces, popcorn, pasta, scrambled tofu, soups, and salad dressings.

**Pro tip:** Add 2–3 tablespoons to a blended cashew cream sauce for a convincing mac and cheese that will fool even non-vegans.

[listicle:Tahini|🌻|Liquid gold for sauces & dressings|/images/tahini-date-energy-balls.jpg]

Made from ground sesame seeds, tahini is one of the most versatile ingredients in the vegan kitchen. It's rich in healthy fats, calcium, and iron, with a slightly bitter, nutty flavour that plays well with both sweet and savoury dishes.

**Use it in:** Hummus, salad dressings, noodle sauces, baked goods, and drizzled over roasted vegetables.

**Pro tip:** Thin it with lemon juice, garlic, and water for the quickest, most satisfying sauce you'll ever make.

[listicle:Canned Legumes|🫘|Instant protein, zero effort|/images/maesri-paste-weeknight-curry.jpg]

Black beans, chickpeas, cannellini beans, lentils — canned legumes are the backbone of quick vegan cooking. They're pre-cooked, affordable, shelf-stable, and packed with plant protein and fibre.

**Use it in:** Curries, tacos, soups, burgers, salads, and dips.

**Pro tip:** Don't drain the liquid from chickpeas — aquafaba whips into a meringue and works as an egg white substitute in countless baking recipes.

[listicle:Miso Paste|🍜|Fermented flavour depth in a spoonful|/images/quick-tofu-sesame-stir-fry.jpg]

Miso is fermented soybean paste and one of the most flavour-dense ingredients you can keep in your fridge. White miso is mild and sweet; red miso is deeper and saltier. Both add an instant savoury richness that builds complexity in minutes.

**Use it in:** Soups, marinades, glazes, salad dressings, and stirred into mashed potatoes or pasta sauces.

**Pro tip:** Never boil miso — add it after removing the pot from heat to preserve its probiotic benefits and keep the flavour bright.

[listicle:Coconut Milk|🥥|Creamy richness without dairy|/images/creamy-vegan-saffron-risotto.jpg]

Full-fat canned coconut milk is a game changer for vegan cooking. It provides the rich, creamy body that dairy cream would in traditional cooking, and it works across the full flavour spectrum — from savoury curries to sweet puddings.

**Use it in:** Curries, soups, smoothies, overnight oats, whipped cream, and ice cream.

**Pro tip:** Refrigerate an unopened can overnight — the cream separates to the top and whips into a thick, dairy-free whipped cream.

[listicle:Soy Sauce or Tamari|🥢|Salt with complexity|/images/quick-vegan-peanut-butter-ramen.jpg]

Soy sauce adds saltiness plus umami, fermented depth, and colour to dishes. Tamari is the gluten-free alternative, with a slightly richer, less sharp flavour. Both are essential for Asian-inspired dishes and work brilliantly as a general seasoning tool.

**Use it in:** Stir-fries, marinades, fried rice, noodles, dipping sauces, and soups.

**Pro tip:** A splash of soy sauce in tomato-based pasta sauce deepens the flavour without making it taste remotely Asian — it just makes it taste *more*.

[listicle:Cashews|🌰|The secret to creamy everything|/images/creamy-vegan-potato-leek-soup.jpg]

Raw cashews, when soaked and blended, produce one of the most convincing dairy substitutes in vegan cooking. They have a neutral flavour and smooth texture that forms the base of vegan cheeses, cream sauces, and desserts.

**Use it in:** Cream sauces, cheesecake, sour cream, ricotta, alfredo, and nut butters.

**Pro tip:** Soak cashews in boiling water for 30 minutes if you're in a hurry — you'll get the same creamy blend as an overnight soak.

[listicle:Apple Cider Vinegar|🍎|Brightness and tang on demand|/images/fresh-vegan-no-cook-gazpacho.jpg]

ACV brings acidity that lifts and balances flavours in vegan cooking — especially important since animal fats naturally add richness that can mask blandness. A small splash can completely transform a flat-tasting dish.

**Use it in:** Salad dressings, slaws, marinades, pickles, baked goods, and vegan buttermilk.

**Pro tip:** Mix 1 tablespoon ACV with 1 cup of plant milk and let it curdle for 5 minutes — instant vegan buttermilk for fluffy pancakes or biscuits.

[listicle:Canned Tomatoes|🍅|The base of a hundred meals|/images/multi-meal-prep-vegan-chili.jpg]

Crushed, diced, or whole peeled — canned tomatoes are the most dependable building block in plant-based cooking. They're harvested at peak ripeness and often more flavourful than fresh tomatoes out of season.

**Use it in:** Pasta sauces, curries, chilis, shakshuka, soups, and braises.

**Pro tip:** Add a pinch of sugar and a small piece of carrot when simmering tomato sauce — it balances acidity naturally without overpowering the tomato flavour.

[listicle:Whole Grains & Pasta|🌾|Hearty foundations that keep you full|/images/pillowy-vegan-focaccia-bread.jpg]

Brown rice, quinoa, farro, oats, and whole grain pasta provide the complex carbohydrates and fibre that make vegan meals genuinely satisfying. They're also excellent vehicles for bold sauces and toppings.

**Use it in:** Grain bowls, stir-fries, porridge, stuffed vegetables, salads, and as a side to any protein-forward main.

**Pro tip:** Cook a large batch of grains on Sunday and refrigerate — you'll have the base for fast weeknight meals all week long.
